bibliographic_data.title: STEM: A Stochastic Two-Sided Momentum Algorithm Achieving Near-Optimal Sample and Communication Complexities for Federated Learning
bibliographic_data.abstract: Federated Learning (FL) refers to the paradigm where multiple worker nodes (WNs) build a joint model by using local data. Despite extensive research, for a generic non-convex FL problem, it is not clear how to choose the WNs’ and the server’s update directions, the minibatch sizes, and the local update frequency, so that the WNs use the minimum number of samples and communication rounds to achieve the desired solution. This work addresses the above question and considers a class of stochastic algorithms where the WNs perform a few local updates before communication. We show that when both the WN’s and the server’s directions are chosen based on a stochastic momentum estimator, the algorithm requires ˜O(ϵ−3/2) samples and ˜O(ϵ−1) communication rounds to compute an ϵ-stationary solution. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first FL algorithm that achieves such near-optimal sample and communication complexities simultaneously.

core_content.novelty_claim: The proposed STEM algorithm utilizes a momentum-assisted stochastic gradient direction for both the WNs and SN updates, achieving near-optimal sample and communication complexities.
core_content.key_methodology: STEM uses stochastic momentum estimators to determine update directions. It requires ˜O(ϵ−3/2) samples and ˜O(ϵ−1) communication rounds to achieve an ϵ-stationary solution.
outcomes: Achieves near-optimal sample and communication complexities for FL algorithms
Provides a trade-off curve between local update frequencies and minibatch sizes
core_content.full_text_markdown: In Federated Learning (FL), multiple worker nodes collaborate to learn a joint model using local data. The paper addresses the challenge of choosing optimal parameters such as update directions, minibatch sizes, and local update frequency for achieving near-optimal sample and communication complexities. The proposed STEM algorithm uses stochastic momentum estimators for both WNs and server updates, requiring ˜O(ϵ−3/2) samples and ˜O(ϵ−1) communication rounds to achieve an ϵ-stationary solution. This is the first FL algorithm that simultaneously achieves these optimal complexities. Additionally, a trade-off curve between local update frequencies and minibatch sizes is provided.
